title: self: &Self vs &self syntax inconsistency throughout spec
description: The spec uses two different syntaxes for self-receivers: 'fn fmt(self: &Self, ...)' and 'fn push(&mut self, ...)'. Both appear in ferrum-lang-generics.md (compare §2.2 trait definitions with §2.8 contract inheritance). The traits introduction exclusively uses the verbose self: &Self form. The stdlib-core.md uses &self. If both are valid, the spec must say so and explain when to use each. If only one is canonical, the other must be removed from the spec. As-is, a developer writing their first trait method has no guidance on which form to write — and will see contradictory examples. The self: &Self form is also notably more verbose than Rust's &self on every single method. If it's the only form, that is a consistent annotation burden the spec should acknowledge and defend.
close_reason: both forms documented as valid sugar in ferrum-lang-syntax.md §3.3: 'self in method position is sugar: self: &Self can be written &self'. The spec is internally consistent. No change needed.
